facts_disclosed: Wife Akosua, 4 children (Kwabena 17, Ama 14, Yaa 10, Kofi Jr 6)
Sells through Akuafo agent, 800 cedis/bag, 3-year contract
Sells all cocoa through cooperative system

turns.content: good evening. i want to ask something about cocoa drying. the rains have not stopped and my beans are not drying well on the mat

Good evening! Wet weather during drying is a real challenge. A few questions to help — are you sun-drying on raised mats or on the ground? And how long have the beans been fermenting before you put them out?
raised mat yes. fermentation 6 days like always. but the sun comes for 2 hours then rain again. beans staying wet too long. i am worried about mould

6 days fermentation is solid. The problem is definitely the inconsistent sun. When beans stay wet for too long after fermentation, you're right to worry — mould and off-flavours develop fast.

A few things you can do:

1. **Cover and uncover** — keep a tarp or plastic sheet ready. When rain comes, cover immediately. Every hour of direct sun counts.
2. **Spread thinner** — if you're drying in thick layers, spread the beans out more. Thinner layers dry faster even with limited sun.
3. **Turn more frequently** — stir the beans every 30-45 minutes when the sun is out. Gets more even airflow.
4. **Raised platform helps** — you already have that, which is good. Air circulating underneath makes a big difference.

How many bags are you trying to dry right now?

about 15 bags worth of wet beans. it is a lot my friend. my wife Akosua and the children are helping turn them but the weather is against us. Kwabena my eldest, he is 17, he is strong. Ama is 14 and she works hard too. even Yaa who is 10 helps. only Kofi Jr is too small, he is 6, he just plays near the mat

That's a proper family effort! 15 bags of wet beans is a big batch to manage in this weather. Good that you have hands helping — turning that volume regularly is tiring work.

With the whole family working the mats, here's what I'd prioritize:

- Assign someone to **watch the sky** and be ready with the tarp. Even 10 minutes of rain on exposed beans sets you back.
- Have Kwabena and Ama focus on **turning** — they can cover more area faster.
- If you can split the batch, dry in **two or three separate mats** so each layer is thinner. Faster drying per batch.

Are you drying these for a buyer who's coming soon, or do you have some time?

the buyer comes when we call. we sell through the cooperative. all of us in the village sell through the cooperative system. the Akuafo agent collects from us. we have 3 year contract with them. 800 cedis per bag of 64kg

Okay, so you have some flexibility on timing since the agent comes when you're ready — that's good. No need to rush and risk putting out poorly dried beans.

At 800 cedis per 64kg bag, quality matters. If the moisture content is too high when the agent checks, they'll either reject the bag or try to negotiate a lower price. The target is around 7-8% moisture for well-dried cocoa.

Since you're not on a tight deadline:

1. **Take your time** — better to dry over 10-14 days in broken sunshine than to rush and deliver wet beans.
2. **The smell test** — properly dried beans snap when you break them and smell chocolatey, not sour or musty.
3. **Night storage** — bring the beans in every evening. Dew is as bad as rain for adding moisture back.

Do you have a covered shed where you can store the beans overnight?
yes we have the drying shed. my father built it. we bring them in every night. the problem is just the daytime rain. this season the rain is confusing. it comes at any hour

Your father planned well with that shed. And you're doing the right things — fermentation is good, raised mats, bringing them in at night.

The unpredictable rain is the one thing you can't control. A couple more ideas:

- **Solar dryer** — some farmers build a simple frame with clear plastic sheeting over the drying mat, like a greenhouse tunnel. Lets sunlight through but keeps rain off. You can make one from bamboo poles and plastic sheet for very little cost. It traps heat too, so beans dry faster.
- **Check the radio weather forecast** each morning — even a rough idea of when rain is expected helps you plan the day.

With 15 bags at 800 cedis each, that's 12,000 cedis worth of cocoa on those mats. Worth protecting properly.
the plastic tunnel is a good idea my friend. Kwabena and I can build that in one morning. bamboo is everywhere here. i will try this

Perfect — bamboo and plastic sheeting is all you need. A few tips for building it:

- Make the tunnel **high enough** to get airflow underneath the plastic. At least 1 metre above the beans.
- Leave the **ends open** or partially open — you need air circulation or the moisture has nowhere to go. A closed tunnel traps humidity and defeats the purpose.
- Angle the plastic so **rain runs off** to one side, not pooling on top.

Kwabena at 17 is the right age for this kind of work. You and him can knock it out quickly.

yes. he is learning the farm. all my children learn the farm. it is how my father taught me. Akosua manages the house and also helps during harvest. we are a farming family my friend. this is what we know

That's how strong farms survive — everyone knows their role and the knowledge passes down. Your children are learning something valuable that no school teaches.

So your plan for tomorrow:
1. Build the bamboo and plastic tunnel over the main drying mat
2. Keep turning the beans whenever the sun is out
3. Aim for that 7-8% moisture before you call the Akuafo agent

With the tunnel and your family working the mats, you'll get those 15 bags dried properly even with this difficult rain. Take the time to do it right — 800 cedis per bag is worth the patience.
medaase. you have helped me. Nyame willing the sun will come more this week. if not, we build the tunnel. i will let you know how it goes my friend

You're welcome! I hope the sun cooperates, but either way the tunnel is a good investment for this season and future ones. Your beans sound like they've had proper care from fermentation onwards — they'll turn out well.

Good luck to you and the family. Come back anytime.
